title: Fitting a chimney balloon
subtitle: Sealing an unused fireplace flue with an inflatable draught stopper
excerpt: An open chimney flue in an unlit fireplace is a direct path for warm air to leave the house. A chimney balloon seals the flue from inside and is removable when you want to use the fireplace. It pays back in the first heating season.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guidance. Chimney Balloon manufacturer specifications for sizing (available in standard UK flue sizes). gov.uk Carbon Monoxide guidance for the safety requirements around sealed flues.

content.text: An open chimney in a room that is not in use draws warm air up continuously by stack effect: the warm flue creates a slight negative pressure at hearth level, pulling room air in and up. A chimney balloon is a double-layer plastic bladder that inflates in the flue throat and seals the opening mechanically, without blocking the flue permanently. Pull the tag to deflate it instantly before lighting a fire.

attrs.body: An open chimney in a 20°C room loses roughly 40-80 W to stack-effect ventilation continuously on a cold day. That is 600-1000 kWh over a five-month heating season. The balloon is inexpensive; payback is inside the first winter.
attrs.title: What this saves

attrs.body: Use only in a flue confirmed as disconnected from all gas appliances.
attrs.title: Carbon monoxide safety

content.text: Measure the flue throat

Reach up inside the fireplace opening and measure the throat: the narrowed section of the flue directly above the smoke shelf, typically 200-400 mm above the grate level. UK chimneys are mostly rectangular at the throat; the common sizes are 225 mm x 450 mm, 225 mm x 375 mm, and 225 mm x 300 mm. Balloons come in standard sizes labelled by those dimensions; buy the size that matches your measurement or the next size up.

Take a torch. The throat is often sooty and may have a throat restrictor plate or a damper. If there is a damper, check whether it is stuck open or can be closed. A working damper is better than a balloon and you should use it. A damper that is corroded open or missing is where the balloon steps in.
Fitting the balloon
Step 1, clear loose soot.
Brush any loose deposits off the throat walls. Loose soot on the seating surface will prevent the balloon from gripping and may cause it to drop.
Step 2, position the balloon in the throat.

Insert the deflated balloon into the throat so the inflation tube hangs down into the firebox. Some designs have a small cardboard tray that sits at the hearth level - this helps position the balloon correctly.
Step 3, inflate until snug.

Blow through the inflation tube in short puffs and check after each that the balloon is expanding evenly. It should contact all four walls of the throat. Stop inflating when resistance is felt; over-inflation damages the balloon and can crack soft mortar in the throat.
Step 4, secure the reminder.

The balloon comes with a cardboard or plastic reminder tab that says "REMOVE BEFORE LIGHTING". Clip or tape this tag visibly at the mouth of the fireplace, or fix it to the mantelpiece. The balloon deflates and can be pulled down in seconds, but only if the user remembers it is there.
Checking the seal

On a cold day, hold the back of your hand at the fireplace opening. Before the balloon, you should have felt a slight downward pull of cool air at the top of the opening and a slight warmth of rising air at the bottom. After fitting, this should stop. A lighted incense stick held at the opening is a more reliable test: the smoke should drift horizontally into the room rather than being drawn up the chimney.
Maintenance and replacement

A balloon typically lasts 3-5 years before the plastic degrades and it loses pressure. Check it annually at the start of the heating season; if it has deflated it has either leaked or been removed and not replaced. The cost of replacement is low enough that replacing on a schedule is sensible.
A
draught-stripping

alternative, if you have closed the fireplace for good, is a solid insulated board cut to the opening and held by friction or fixed screws. This is more airtight than a balloon and more permanent. It also hides the firebox, which some owners prefer.

items.fix: Check the tube valve closes fully. Run a torch up the throat to inspect for protruding brick or mortar edges before reinserting.
items.cause: Inflation tube not sealed after blowing, or balloon punctured on a sharp edge in the throat
items.symptom: Balloon deflates within hours of fitting

items.fix: Remove, re-inflate larger if size allows, or try a different approach (sealed board). Address other draughts in parallel.
items.cause: Balloon not sealing the full throat perimeter, or there is a second source of draughts (floor gaps, window frames)
items.symptom: Room still feels draughty after fitting

items.fix: Clean the throat walls more thoroughly. If the surface is too rough, a solid board over the fireplace opening is more reliable.
items.cause: Throat walls too irregular or sooty for the balloon to grip
